text: Welcome to this video presentation. We would like to demonstrate how additional length for allele pouch anal endostomosis can be obtained to make the pouch reach without tension. At the same time, we aim to demonstrate the potential value of fluorescence angiography in this setting to assert an adequate perfusion.

The case we would like to present regards a twenty-three year old female patient diagnosed with ulcerative colitis in two thousand and thirteen. Due to biological refractory disease, she underwent a subtotal colectomy and ileostomy procedure in May, two thousand and twenty. After convalescence, she was planned for a modified two-stage pouch procedure.
One of the Achilles heels of pouch surgery is anastomotic leakage. Insufficient reach of the pouch is a risk factor for developing anastomotic leakage due to traction on the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs routine intraoperative lengthening measures by mobilizing the mesenteric root of the small bowel up to the duodenum and making transverse peritoneal incisions approximately every three centimeters. A flowchart is used to represent the pathway of these lengthening measures, aiming for the pouch to reach two centimeters below the lower border of the pubic bone.

If these routine lengthening measures do not suffice to obtain adequate reach, additional lengthening measures are required. Options available to the surgeon are ligation of the ileocolic vascular trunk or ligating the interconnecting terminal ileobranches. Taking vascular branches to obtain additional length has the inherent risk to compromise the perfusion of the pouch by either compromising inflow or outflow.

The terminal ileum is exteriorized through the old ileostomy site. White arrows indicate traction caused by the interconnecting terminal ileal branches. Transillumination reveals the vascular arcade within the terminal ileal mesentery. The surgeon assesses macroscopic perfusion of the terminal ileum by clamping the interconnecting terminal ileal branches before ligation.

The surgeon ligates the interconnecting terminal ileal branches to obtain additional mesenteric length, allowing the pouch to reach its intended position. This action risks impairing the arterial supply and perfusion of the terminal ileum.

The surgeon assesses the perfusion of the newly created pouch using fluorescence angiography with an NDC nine green bolus. The fluorescent overlay image reveals suboptimal perfusion at the tip of the pouch, which is reinforced with separate PDS-IV-Zero sutures.

The video shows a preoperative plan modification from a two-stage to a three-stage procedure, including the creation of a protective ileostomy. Two weeks postoperatively, endoluminal assessment images reveal a well-vascularized and intact blind loop on the left and the anastomotic site on the right.
